statement.en: MicroRNAs-mediated environmental adaptation in marine microalgae: from physiological responses to biotechnology applications
statement.summary: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs that can regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally by interacting with mRNAs. They are fine-tune modulators that have a pleiotropic effect, interacting with different targets. Thus, they are deeply embedded in gene regulatory networks. Indeed, their modulation can directly or indirectly regulate pathways without energetically stress the cell. Although, their importance is well known and they are widely investigated in human, mouse, and other model organisms, they remain poorly studied in marine organism, even in models such as Phaeodactylum tricornutum. Therefore, we wanted to identify and characterize miRNAs in this diatom, observing their differential expression under three different stress conditions: low salinity, nitrogen limit

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: This is a preprint (dated 2026-04-25, future date suggesting provisional/staged submission) describing empirical research on miRNA characterization in marine microalgae under stress conditions. The work presents experimental findings not yet peer-reviewed or published in final form. The abstract is incomplete (cuts off mid-sentence at 'nitrogen limit'), indicating draft status. Claims about miRNA function are established in model organisms but novel applications to marine diatoms remain under investigation.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 小さな調律者たちが、環境の揺らぎに対して多元的な経路を静かに抑制する。
